1: 'A word was revealed to Daniel ... and the word was true, and it was about a great conflict.' The 'great conflict' (ṣābāʾ gādôl, great warfare/service) is the cosmic backdrop of the entire vision — the spiritual war behind earthly events. Paul names this same conflict in Ephesians 6:12: 'We do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers over this present darkness.' Christ is revealed in Daniel's vision as the one who presides over this conflict and whose victory determines its outcome.
2: Daniel mourns for three full weeks — the same period during which the heavenly messenger battles the prince of Persia (Daniel 10:13). The three weeks of sustained mourning become the context of revelation. Jesus taught his disciples to persist in prayer without losing heart (Luke 18:1-8: 'pray always and not lose heart'); the widow's persistent plea before the unjust judge is the picture of persistent intercession that Daniel models here.
3: Daniel eats no pleasant food, no meat or wine, and does not anoint himself for three weeks. The combination of dietary abstinence, non-anointing, and mourning constitutes a sustained Lenten posture. Christ fasts forty days in the wilderness (Matthew 4:2) — a comparable season of spiritual preparation before the decisive conflict. In both cases, physical self-denial creates the receptive posture for divine revelation.
4: The vision comes by the Tigris on the twenty-fourth day of the first month. The first month is Nisan — the Passover month. On the fourteenth of Nisan the Passover lamb is sacrificed; ten days later Daniel receives this vision of the glorious man. The Passover-month context frames the vision: the imagery of the glorious deliverer received in the month of deliverance connects to Christ as the true Passover lamb (1 Corinthians 5:7: 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed').
5: 'Behold, a man clothed in linen, with a belt of fine gold from Uphaz around his waist.' The man clothed in linen — the priestly garment (Leviticus 16:4; Ezekiel 9:2-3) — is the central figure of the vision. This description is the direct source for John's vision of the risen Christ in Revelation 1:13: 'one like a son of man, clothed with a long robe and with a golden sash around his chest.' The glory-figure of Daniel 10 becomes the glorified Christ of Revelation — whether this is an angel or a pre-incarnate Christophany, Revelation's identification of the same imagery with the ascended Jesus is deliberate.
6: 'His body was like beryl, his face like the appearance of lightning, his eyes like flaming torches, his arms and legs like the gleam of burnished bronze, and the sound of his words like the sound of a multitude.' The four-part description — face like lightning, eyes like fire, limbs like burnished bronze, voice like many waters — is reproduced almost verbatim in Revelation 1:14-15 for the glorified Christ. The man of Daniel 10 is the visual vocabulary John uses to depict the risen Lord. However this figure is identified in Daniel's vision, Revelation declares: the man you saw, Daniel — that is Christ glorified.
7: The other men with Daniel do not see the vision, yet a great trembling falls on them and they flee to hide. Only Daniel remains, seeing the vision alone. This parallels the Transfiguration: only Peter, James, and John witness the glory of Christ (Matthew 17:1-8); the other disciples wait below. The private revelation of divine glory — overwhelming those who are near even if they cannot see directly — is the experiential pattern of encounters with the holy.
8: 'I was left alone and saw this great vision, and no strength was left in me. My radiant appearance was fearfully changed, and I retained no strength.' The encounter with divine glory strips away all human self-sufficiency. Paul's 'when I am weak, then I am strong' (2 Corinthians 12:10) is the NT articulation of this principle: only when Daniel's own strength is utterly depleted can he receive the strength the angel will give. Christ's kenōsis (Philippians 2:7: 'emptied himself, taking the form of a servant') is the divine counterpart — the Son setting aside the exercise of divine prerogatives in order to enter fully into human weakness.
9: Daniel falls into a deep sleep when he hears the voice. The 'deep sleep' (tardēmāh) that falls on Daniel echoes Adam's deep sleep from which the bride was formed (Genesis 2:21). Paul develops the same imagery: 'Husbands, love your wives, as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her' (Ephesians 5:25-32) — the covenant-sleep of the bridegroom through whose self-giving the bride is created. The prophet's collapse before the divine voice is the pattern for the apostle's (Revelation 1:17: 'When I saw him, I fell at his feet as though dead').
10: 'And behold, a hand touched me and set me trembling on my hands and knees.' The restorative hand that raises Daniel from the ground — the gentle counter-action to the overwhelming divine presence. This motif of the touching hand appears three times in Daniel 10 (Daniel 10:10, 16, 18) and once in Daniel 9:21. In Matthew 8:3, Jesus 'stretched out his hand and touched' the leper — the same restorative, equalizing touch from the holy to the unclean. The angel's hand in Daniel anticipates the hands of Christ, always reaching down to raise those who have fallen before the holy.
11: 'O Daniel, man greatly loved, understand the words that I speak to you, and stand upright, for now I have been sent to you.' The address 'man greatly loved' (ʾîš ḥămûdôt) — the second time Daniel receives this designation (cf. Daniel 9:23). The command to 'stand upright' (ʿemod) before the messenger is the posture of readiness for service. Jesus's restorative commands follow the same pattern: to the paralytic, 'Get up and walk' (Matthew 9:5); to the disciples prostrate at the Transfiguration, 'Rise, and have no fear' (Matthew 17:7). The divine summons to stand upright is always a summons to renewed agency in God's service.
12: 'From the first day that you set your heart to understand and humbled yourself before your God, your words have been heard, and I have come because of your words.' Prayer is heard from its beginning — not from the moment of answer but from the moment of petition. Hebrews 7:25 declares: 'He is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them.' Christ intercedes continuously, not waiting for repeated petitions. The three weeks of apparent unanswered prayer (Daniel 10:2-3) concealed an answer already dispatched from the first day.
13: 'The prince of the kingdom of Persia withstood me twenty-one days, but Michael, one of the chief princes, came to help me.' The angelic battle behind earthly history reveals a cosmic dimension to political events. Ephesians 6:12 explicitly names 'the rulers, the authorities, the cosmic powers over this present darkness' as the actual adversaries in the spiritual war. Christ's victory over these powers at the cross is the definitive resolution of this conflict: 'He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15).
14: 'I have come to make you understand what is to happen to your people in the latter days. For the vision is for days yet to come.' The angel's mission is to reveal the eschatological future of Daniel's people. Hebrews 1:2 declares that God 'in these last days has spoken to us by his Son' — the 'latter days' to which the vision points have now arrived in Christ. The Son is the final word after all the prophetic messengers; the vision Daniel receives points forward to the same events that Jesus addresses in the Olivet Discourse (Matt 24).
15: Daniel is struck dumb, his face toward the ground, unable to speak. The encounter with the divine presence has robbed him of speech — the same speechlessness that strikes Zechariah before Gabriel (Luke 1:20), and the same prostration that flattens John before the risen Christ (Revelation 1:17). The inability to speak in the presence of holiness is not failure but appropriate response; the restoration of speech that follows is itself a divine gift. Christ restores speech to the mute (Matthew 9:32-33) and to Zechariah (Luke 1:64) — the physical healings are enacted theology.
16: One in the form of a son of man touches Daniel's lips and restores his speech: 'O my lord, by reason of the vision pains have come upon me, and I retain no strength.' The touch on Daniel's lips mirrors Isaiah's burning coal (Isaiah 6:7: 'your guilt is taken away, your sin atoned for') and YHWH's touch on Jeremiah's mouth (Jeremiah 1:9: 'I have put my words in your mouth'). In each case, the prophetic voice is given not possessed; speech about God is enabled by God. Christ — the Word who enables the prophetic word — is both the content of the speech and the one who makes it possible.
17: 'How can my lord's servant talk with my lord? For now no strength remains in me, and no breath is left in me.' The rhetorical question — how can I speak before you? — is the radical incompetence of the human before the divine. Christ is both the one before whom this incompetence is real and the one who overcomes it: 'the Spirit helps us in our weakness. For we do not know what to pray for as we ought, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words' (Romans 8:26). The breath that Daniel lacks, the Spirit supplies.
18: Again one having the appearance of a man touches Daniel and strengthens him. The triple strengthening (Daniel 10:10, 16, 18) is the persistent, patient work of the divine messenger in the face of persistent weakness. Jesus strengthens his disciples after the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:7) and in Gethsemane an angel comes to strengthen him (Luke 22:43). The pattern of divine strength administered through touch in moments of exhaustion runs from Daniel through the Gospels to the promise: 'My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness' (2 Corinthians 12:9).
19: 'O man greatly loved, fear not, peace be with you; be strong and of good courage.' The threefold word — 'fear not, peace, be strong' — is the angelic benediction that prepares Daniel for the vision to follow. The risen Christ greets his disciples with the same sequence: 'Peace be with you' (John 20:19, 21, 26), 'Do not be afraid' (Matthew 28:10), 'Take heart, I have overcome the world' (John 16:33). The angel's word to Daniel is the prototype of the risen Lord's word to his church — the same peace, the same courage, the same 'greatly loved' as the ground of the greeting.
20: 'Do you know why I have come to you? But now I will return to fight against the prince of Persia; and when I go out, behold, the prince of Greece will come.' The heavenly being reveals the cosmic succession: the prince of Persia will yield to the prince of Greece. Earthly imperial succession has a spiritual dimension — each empire is animated by a governing spiritual power. Christ's victory at the cross is understood in Revelation as the definitive overthrow of 'the ruler of this world' (John 12:31): 'Now is the judgment of this world; now will the ruler of this world be cast out.' The series of imperial 'princes' Daniel describes ends in Christ's triumph.
21: 'But I will tell you what is inscribed in the book of truth: there is none who contends by my side against these except Michael, your prince.' The 'book of truth' (ketāb ʾĕmet) — the heavenly register of what will certainly come to pass. Revelation 5 introduces the sealed scroll of God's purposes, which only the Lamb is worthy to open: what is inscribed in Daniel's book of truth is unsealed by Christ. Michael is Israel's protector (Daniel 12:1); Christ is the church's high priest-king. The same heavenly book opens in Revelation — in Christ's hands — to reveal the outcome that Daniel could only glimpse.
